topicTitle: System Design Fundamentals — Scalability, Caching & Load Balancing
question: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling?
quickAnswer: Vertical scaling (scale up): add more resources to a single machine (bigger CPU, more RAM). Simple but has a physical limit and single point of failure. Horizontal scaling (scale out): add more machines to distribute load. No single point of failure, theoretically unlimited, but requires stateless design and adds complexity (load balancing, data consistency, distributed state).
keyPoints:
Vertical: single machine, bigger specs — simple, limited, SPOF
Horizontal: more machines — complex but no theoretical limit
Stateless requirement: horizontal scaling requires no local state (use Redis for sessions)
Auto-scaling: horizontal scaling can be automated (AWS ASG)
Database scaling: vertical easy; horizontal (sharding) very complex
Cost: vertical is linear; horizontal can be cheaper at scale with commodity hardware

topicTitle: System Design Fundamentals — Scalability, Caching & Load Balancing
question: What are the main caching strategies and when do you use each?
quickAnswer: Cache-aside (lazy loading): app checks cache first, loads from DB on miss, populates cache. Cache-through: cache layer handles all DB reads/writes. Write-through: write to cache and DB synchronously — consistent but slower writes. Write-behind (write-back): write to cache first, async write to DB — fast but risk of data loss. Read-through + write-through: app only talks to cache, cache manages DB.
keyPoints:
Cache-aside: most common — app controls cache population; cold start misses
Write-through: cache + DB written together — consistent, slower writes
Write-behind: write cache only, async DB flush — fast, risk of loss on crash
Read-through: cache fetches from DB on miss automatically
Eviction policies: LRU, LFU, TTL-based, random
Cache invalidation: hardest problem — TTL + event-based invalidation

topicTitle: System Design Fundamentals — Scalability, Caching & Load Balancing
question: What is consistent hashing and why is it used in distributed systems?
quickAnswer: Consistent hashing maps both servers and keys onto a virtual ring. Each key goes to the next server clockwise on the ring. When a server is added/removed, only K/N keys (where K=total keys, N=servers) are remapped — not all keys. Traditional modulo hashing remaps nearly all keys on resize. Used in: CDN (Akamai), distributed caches, load balancers, Cassandra, DynamoDB.
keyPoints:
Problem: modulo hashing (key % n) remaps ~K*(N-1)/N keys when n changes
Consistent hashing: ring-based, only K/N keys remapped on server add/remove
Virtual nodes: each server has multiple positions on ring — improves balance
Hotspot prevention: virtual nodes distribute load more evenly
Used in: Cassandra, Redis Cluster, CDN routing, Memcached (twemproxy)
Replication: next M servers clockwise hold replicas

topicTitle: System Design Fundamentals — Scalability, Caching & Load Balancing
question: What is the difference between L4 and L7 load balancing?
quickAnswer: L4 load balancing (transport layer): routes based on IP + TCP/UDP port — fast, no packet inspection. L7 load balancing (application layer): routes based on HTTP content (URL path, headers, cookies) — enables path-based routing, SSL termination, session stickiness, A/B testing. AWS ALB is L7; NLB is L4. L7 adds latency but enables intelligent routing.
keyPoints:
L4 (NLB): TCP/UDP level, IP+port routing, very fast, no content inspection
L7 (ALB): HTTP/HTTPS, path/header-based routing, SSL termination
Path-based: /api → API servers, /static → CDN
Session stickiness (sticky sessions): route user to same server via cookie
Health checks: L7 checks HTTP 200; L4 checks TCP connection
ALB vs NLB: ALB for web apps; NLB for lowest latency, static IP, non-HTTP

topicTitle: Authentication System Design — JWT, SSO & Refresh Tokens
question: How does JWT authentication differ from session-based authentication?
quickAnswer: Session-based: server stores session data in memory/DB and sends session ID cookie. Stateful — every request needs a DB/cache lookup. JWT: client holds the token with encoded claims. Stateless — server only validates signature. JWT scales horizontally without shared session store. Tradeoff: JWTs can't be immediately revoked (until expiry), while sessions can be deleted from server instantly.
keyPoints:
Session: server-side state (DB/Redis), cookie with session ID, easy revocation
JWT: client-side token, no server state, scales easily, stateless
JWT revocation problem: can't invalidate before expiry without a denylist (negates stateless benefit)
Solution: short-lived access tokens (15min) + long-lived refresh tokens
Access token: JWT, stateless, short expiry
Refresh token: opaque, stored in DB, can be revoked

topicTitle: Authentication System Design — JWT, SSO & Refresh Tokens
question: What is SSO (Single Sign-On) and how does OAuth2/OIDC enable it?
quickAnswer: SSO allows users to authenticate once and access multiple applications. OAuth2 is an authorization framework; OIDC (OpenID Connect) adds authentication on top. Flow: user clicks login → redirect to Identity Provider (IdP) → user authenticates → IdP issues authorization code → app exchanges code for access_token + id_token. id_token is a JWT with user identity claims.
keyPoints:
SSO: authenticate once (IdP), access many apps (SPs)
OAuth2: authorization (permission delegation), not authentication
OIDC: OAuth2 + id_token (JWT) + /userinfo endpoint = authentication
Authorization Code flow: redirect → code → token exchange (server-side)
PKCE: Proof Key for Code Exchange — for SPAs/mobile (no client secret)
Tokens: access_token (resource access), id_token (user identity), refresh_token (renew)

topicTitle: Authentication System Design — JWT, SSO & Refresh Tokens
question: How do you design a refresh token system?
quickAnswer: Access tokens are short-lived (15-30min) JWTs. Refresh tokens are long-lived (7-30 days), opaque (random UUID), stored in DB. On access token expiry, client sends refresh token to get a new access token. Server validates refresh token exists and is not revoked. Implement rotation: issue new refresh token on each use, invalidate the old one. Detect reuse of revoked refresh token as possible theft.
keyPoints:
Access token: JWT, 15-30 min, stateless
Refresh token: opaque UUID, 7-30 days, stored in DB, can be revoked
Rotation: new refresh token on each use — detect reuse of old one
Token family: if old token reused, revoke entire family (theft signal)
Storage: access in memory/local storage; refresh in httpOnly cookie
Revocation: delete refresh token from DB → user must re-login
Silent refresh: SPA silently requests new access token before expiry

topicTitle: Distributed Systems — CAP Theorem, Sharding & Replication
question: Explain the CAP theorem and give a real-world example.
quickAnswer: CAP theorem: a distributed system can only guarantee two of three: Consistency (all nodes see same data simultaneously), Availability (every request gets a response, no error), Partition tolerance (system works despite network partitions). Since network partitions always happen in distributed systems, you really choose between CP (consistent under partition) and AP (available under partition). Example: Zookeeper/HBase = CP; Cassandra/DynamoDB = AP.
keyPoints:
Consistency: all reads see the latest write
Availability: every request gets a non-error response (might be stale)
Partition tolerance: system continues despite network splits
P is always required: networks partition — pick CP or AP
CP systems: Zookeeper, HBase, Etcd — return error on partition
AP systems: Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB — return possibly stale data
PACELC: extends CAP — also considers latency tradeoff when no partition

topicTitle: Distributed Systems — CAP Theorem, Sharding & Replication
question: What is database sharding and what are the sharding strategies?
quickAnswer: Sharding partitions data horizontally across multiple database servers. Each shard holds a subset of rows. Strategies: Range-based (shard by ID range — simple but hotspots possible), Hash-based (hash(key) % n — even distribution), Directory-based (lookup table maps key to shard — flexible but lookup bottleneck), Geographic (data locality for compliance). Sharding adds complexity: cross-shard queries, joins, transactions are hard.
keyPoints:
Range sharding: shard by ID/date range — simple, hotspot risk (recent data)
Hash sharding: hash(key) % n — even distribution, resharding is expensive
Consistent hashing: hash sharding without full reshuffle on resize
Directory sharding: mapping table — flexible but single point of failure
Cross-shard: JOINs across shards are very expensive (application-level join)
Sharding challenges: distributed transactions, hot shards, schema changes

topicTitle: Distributed Systems — CAP Theorem, Sharding & Replication
question: What is the difference between strong consistency, eventual consistency, and causal consistency?
quickAnswer: Strong consistency: reads always see the latest write — requires coordination, high latency. Eventual consistency: replicas converge to same value eventually — high availability, low latency, temporary stale reads. Causal consistency: causally related writes are seen in order by all nodes — middle ground. Most distributed databases offer eventual consistency with tunable quorum reads for stronger guarantees.
keyPoints:
Strong (linearizability): every read returns latest write — Google Spanner, ZooKeeper
Eventual: replicas converge over time — Cassandra, DynamoDB default
Causal: causally related operations in order — MongoDB sessions
Read-your-writes: see your own writes immediately (session consistency)
Monotonic reads: won't see older data than before
Tunable: Cassandra quorum (R + W > N) — adjust consistency vs availability

topicTitle: Performance Patterns — Circuit Breaker, Retry, Bulkhead & Idempotency
question: How does the Retry pattern work and what is exponential backoff?
quickAnswer: Retry pattern: automatically retry failed requests to handle transient failures. Exponential backoff: wait time doubles with each retry (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s) to reduce pressure on recovering services. Jitter: add random noise to backoff to prevent synchronized retry storms (thundering herd). Stop retrying on non-transient errors (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized).
keyPoints:
Retry: transient failures (network timeout, 503) → retry
Don't retry: 4xx client errors (except 429 rate limit, 408 timeout)
Exponential backoff: wait = base * 2^attempt — reduces thundering herd
Jitter: wait = random(0, base * 2^attempt) — spreads retries
Max retries: limit to 3-5 with timeout — set deadline to avoid infinite retries
Resilience4j: @Retry annotation with customizable backoff strategy

topicTitle: Performance Patterns — Circuit Breaker, Retry, Bulkhead & Idempotency
question: What is the Bulkhead pattern?
quickAnswer: Bulkhead pattern isolates different parts of a system into separate pools/thread groups so a failure in one doesn't consume resources needed by others. Named after ship bulkheads that prevent flooding from spreading. Example: separate thread pool for each downstream service — slow payment service exhausts its own pool without starving inventory service. Prevents cascading failures via resource isolation.
keyPoints:
Isolation: separate thread pools, connection pools, or semaphores per service
Prevents: one slow service consuming all threads and starving others
Types: thread pool isolation (heavyweight), semaphore isolation (lightweight)
Resilience4j Bulkhead: @Bulkhead(name, type=SEMAPHORE) or THREADPOOL
Microservice: each service should have separate HTTP client thread pools
DB connections: separate pool for read queries vs write queries

topicTitle: Performance Patterns — Circuit Breaker, Retry, Bulkhead & Idempotency
question: How do you implement idempotency for APIs?
quickAnswer: Idempotency ensures the same request produces the same result regardless of how many times it's made. Implement with Idempotency-Key header: client generates a unique UUID per logical operation. Server stores (idempotency_key, response) in a store (Redis with TTL). On duplicate: return cached response without re-executing. Critical for payment APIs — prevents double charges on retry.
keyPoints:
Client sends Idempotency-Key: UUID-per-operation (not per request)
Server: check if key seen before → return cached response
Storage: Redis with TTL (key → serialized response)
First request: execute + store; subsequent: return cached
Conflict: same key but different body → 422 or 409
HTTP GET/PUT/DELETE: naturally idempotent; POST needs explicit key
Payment APIs: Stripe, Braintree require Idempotency-Key header

topicTitle: Performance Patterns — Circuit Breaker, Retry, Bulkhead & Idempotency
question: What is rate limiting and what algorithms implement it?
quickAnswer: Rate limiting controls how many requests a client can make in a time window, protecting services from overload and abuse. Algorithms: Fixed Window (simple but burst at boundary), Sliding Window Log (accurate, memory-heavy), Sliding Window Counter (approximate, efficient), Token Bucket (allows bursts, smooth), Leaky Bucket (strict output rate). Redis is commonly used to store counts across distributed instances.
keyPoints:
Fixed window: count per time slot — boundary burst problem
Sliding window log: store request timestamps — accurate, O(requests) memory
Sliding window counter: weighted previous window — good balance
Token bucket: tokens refill at rate; burst allowed if tokens available
Leaky bucket: queue of requests drains at constant rate — no bursts
Distributed: use Redis INCR + EXPIRE or Redis sorted sets for sliding window
